text: The capture and recycling of CO2 into value-added chemicals, such as alcohols, represents a vital strategy for mitigating greenhouse gas emissions. However, the inherent chemical inertness of CO2 remains a significant barrier, making its activation the rate-limiting step in efficient conversion processes. While bulk gold is catalytically inactive for hydrogenation, it can be activated when dispersed as nanoparticles across specific oxide supports. In this combined experimental and theoretical investigation, we demonstrate that electronic polarization at the metal-oxide interface—specifically within Au nanoparticles anchored on a CeOx/TiO2 substrate—creates potent active sites for CO2 adsorption. These interfacial sites facilitate low-pressure hydrogenation with enhanced selectivity toward methanol. Our findings highlight how tailoring localized electronic properties and interfacial structures can drive the development of high-performance catalysts for the selective transformation of CO2 into sustainable fuels.
